tools.title: netexec (nxc, formerly crackmapexec)
tools.purpose: Swiss army knife for domain and network auditing: credential checks, SMB/LDAP enumeration, secrets dumping, command execution over WMI/SMB, and running modules across a host list at once. Direct successor to CrackMapExec.

modes.name: smb
modes.desc: SMB auditing: shares, users, RID-brute, SAM/NTDS dump, command execution.

modes.name: ldap
modes.desc: AD enumeration over LDAP: users, AS-REP/Kerberoast, delegation, BloodHound.

modes.name: winrm
modes.desc: Credential checks and command execution over WinRM (5985/5986).

modes.name: mssql
modes.desc: MSSQL auditing: login, T-SQL execution, and xp_cmdshell.

modes.name: ssh
modes.desc: SSH credential checks and command execution.

modes.name: wmi
modes.desc: Credential checks and command execution over WMI.

flags.desc: Username or a file with a list of names.
Password or a file with a list of passwords (tried against all -u).
NTLM hash for Pass-the-Hash instead of a password.
Authenticate via Kerberos (uses a ticket from the environment / ccache).
Local authentication on the host rather than domain (for local accounts).
Domain for domain authentication.
Address of the KDC/domain controller (needed for Kerberos).
Do not stop after the first valid login - for password spray across a list.
Enumerate network shares and access rights (READ/WRITE) to them.
Enumerate domain users.
Brute-force RIDs to extract users (works even with a null session). Optional max RID.
Retrieve the domain password policy.
Dump local hashes from SAM (requires admin rights).
Dump hashes of all domain accounts from NTDS.dit (on the DC, via DRSUAPI).
Enumerate domain groups (or members of a specific group).
Enumerate local groups on the host.
Show active sessions on the host.
Show logged-on users.
Enumerate disks on the host.
Dump LSA secrets.
Run a command via cmd on the target.
Run a command via PowerShell on the target.
Execution method: wmiexec, atexec, smbexec, mmcexec.
Show the list of available modules for the protocol.
Run a module by name.
Pass options to the module (KEY=value, multiple allowed). List options via --options.
Show options of the selected module (-M ... --options).
Collect AS-REP hashes of users without pre-authentication (to a file).
Collect TGS hashes of service accounts (Kerberoasting, to a file).
Find accounts with unconstrained delegation.
Collect data for BloodHound via LDAP (with --collection).

recipes.note: Check credentials and enumerate available shares on the host.
Extract users via a null session with RID brute.
Password spray a single password across a host/target list.
Pass-the-Hash on the DC and dump NTDS.dit with all domain hashes.
Collect AS-REP hashes of users without pre-authentication.
Run a command on the target (by default via wmiexec/smbexec).

tools.notes: The first argument after nxc is always the protocol (smb/ldap/winrm/mssql/ssh/wmi/rdp/ftp/vnc), then the target (IP/CIDR/file). -u and -p accept files - all pairs are tried automatically. Green (+) means valid credentials, (Pwn3d!) means admin access. Config and logs: ~/.nxc/. Inherits CrackMapExec syntax.

tools.title: enum4linux-ng
tools.purpose: enum4linux rewritten in Python: in a single run it collects users, groups, shares, password policy, OS, RID-cycling from an SMB/RPC host and exports to JSON/YAML. Handy for quick Windows/Samba recon.

flags.desc: Full 'simple' enumeration: users, groups, shares, policy, OS, NetBIOS, printers (-U -G -S -P -O -N -I -L).
Same as -A but without NetBIOS queries.
RID-cycling - brute-forcing RIDs to extract users. Optional batch size.
Verbose output.
Retrieve users via RPC.
Retrieve groups via RPC.
Retrieve groups along with their members via RPC.
Retrieve shares via RPC.
Retrieve the password policy via RPC.
Retrieve OS information.
Retrieve domain information via LDAP/LDAPS.
Retrieve printer information via RPC.
NetBIOS name lookup.
Retrieve services via RPC.
Username (empty by default - null session).
Password (empty by default).
Authentication with an NT hash.
Authentication via a Kerberos ticket (for AD).
Local authentication rather than domain.
Specify the workgroup/domain (auto-detected by default).
Detailed information on users and groups (with -U/-G/-R).
RID ranges to brute-force (default 500-550,1000-1050).
Known existing names to test (comma-separated).
Brute-force shares using a wordlist from a file.
Connection timeout in seconds (default 10).
Export results to JSON (extension added automatically).
Export results to YAML.
Export to both JSON and YAML at once.

recipes.note: Full enumeration with a null session - the standard first run.
Same, but with valid domain credentials (more data).
Enumerate users via RID-cycling over an extended range.
Full run with output saved to result.json and result.yaml.

tools.notes: The target is a positional argument (IP/hostname) at the end of the command. By default it works anonymously (null session). -A covers most needs; targeted flags (-U/-S/...) are handy for noisy targets. Requires the smbclient/samba-common packages (used under the hood).

tools.title: smbclient / smbmap
tools.purpose: smbclient is an FTP-like client for connecting to SMB shares, listing, and downloading/uploading files. smbmap does mass share enumeration with access-rights display, recursive traversal, and command execution.
tools.install: sudo apt install smbclient; pipx install smbmap

flags.desc: Enumerate available shares on the server (//host or host).
SMB port (default 445).
Maximum SMB protocol version (SMB2/SMB3).
Explicitly set the target IP (if the name does not resolve).
Username (can be DOMAIN/user or user%password).
No password (anonymous/null access) - do not prompt for a password.
Treat the password from -U as an NT hash (Pass-the-Hash).
Authenticate via Kerberos.
Workgroup/domain.
Run commands non-interactively (separated by ;): ls, get, put, mget...
Debug level.
Machine-readable (grep-friendly) output.
List files in the current directory of the share.
Download a file from the share.
Upload a file to the share.
Download multiple files by mask (with prompt OFF / recurse ON).
Change to a directory on the share.

recipes.note: Anonymously enumerate shares on the host.
Connect to a specific share with credentials (interactive session).
Anonymously and recursively list the entire contents of the share.
Pass-the-Hash and file download in a single command.
smbmap: recursively show shares and access rights.

tools.notes: smbclient: target in the form //host/share (or -L //host for listing). smbmap (binary smbmap) is a separate tool: -H host (target), -u/-p/-d (creds/domain), -L (all drives), -r [PATH] (directory listing), -R [PATH] (recursive), --download / --upload SRC DST (files), -x 'cmd' (run a command). smbmap is more convenient for a quick overview of rights across all shares at once.

tools.title: rpcclient
tools.purpose: A client for calling MS-RPC functions on Windows/Samba. The main tool for manual domain enumeration via a null session or with credentials: users, groups, SIDs, password policy, server information.

flags.desc: Credentials (user, user%password, or DOMAIN/user).
No password - null session (anonymous).
Workgroup/domain.
Authenticate via Kerberos.
Treat the password from -U as an NT hash (Pass-the-Hash).
Run command(s) non-interactively (separated by ;) instead of an interactive session.
List domain users and their RIDs.
List domain groups and their RIDs.
Users with names, full name, and description (often sees more than enumdomusers).
Detailed information about a user by RID.
A user's groups by their RID.
Retrieve the domain SID and name.
Get the SID for an account name.
Get the account name for a SID (manual RID-cycling).
Domain password policy (minimum length, complexity).
List domains on the server.
Server information (type, OS version).
Enumerate all shares via RPC.
Enumerate printers.
Enumerate SIDs known to LSA.

recipes.note: Open an interactive null session for manual enumeration.
Quickly extract the list of domain users anonymously.
Users with descriptions (sometimes passwords are stored there).
Password policy and domain SID with valid credentials.

tools.notes: The target is a positional argument (IP/hostname). Without -c it opens an interactive rpcclient $> shell where commands are entered one at a time. With -c, commands are separated by ';'. A null session (-U '' -N) is often available on old/misconfigured DCs. The querydispinfo and enumdomusers commands may yield different coverage - try both.

tools.title: ldapsearch
tools.purpose: The standard OpenLDAP client for directory queries. In AD pentesting - extracting users, groups, descriptions, SPNs, and attributes via anonymous or authenticated bind. Precise control over filters and attributes.

flags.desc: Server URI (ldap://host:389 or ldaps://host:636).
Simple authentication (required even for anonymous bind, instead of SASL).
Bind DN - the account for authentication (UPN or full DN).
Password on the command line.
Prompt for the password interactively (do not expose it in history).
Read the password from a file.
Search base (Base DN) - the root to search from.
Search scope: base, one, sub (sub by default).
Limit on the number of records in the response.
Time limit for the search (seconds).
Strip comments and the LDIF version from the output (clean result).
Output options; ldif-wrap=no disables wrapping of long lines.
Query extension; paged results (pr) to bypass the AD limit of 1000 records.
Save binary attribute values to temporary files.
Search filter - the first positional argument after options.
Find all user objects.
Accounts with an SPN - Kerberoasting candidates.
List of attributes to output - after the filter (otherwise all attributes).

recipes.note: Anonymously learn the domain Base DN (RootDSE is almost always accessible).
Enumerate users and their descriptions (anonymously, if bind is allowed).
Full directory dump with valid credentials without line wrapping.
Find accounts with an SPN for Kerberoasting.

tools.notes: AD forbids anonymous search except for RootDSE - credentials (-D/-w) are usually required. The filter and attribute list go POSITIONALLY after all options. For dumping >1000 objects use paged results: -E pr=1000/noprompt. The Base DN can be pulled from RootDSE (-s base -b ''). Related tools: windapsearch, ldapdomaindump.

tools.title: kerbrute
tools.purpose: Fast and relatively quiet brute force via Kerberos pre-auth: username validation, password spray, and brute force. Does not trigger event 4625 on a failed pre-authentication, so it is stealthier than typical methods.

modes.name: userenum
modes.desc: Check the existence of usernames against a wordlist.

modes.name: passwordspray
modes.desc: A single password against a list of users.

modes.name: bruteuser
modes.desc: Brute-force passwords from a wordlist against a single user.

modes.name: bruteforce
modes.desc: Brute-force user:password pairs from a file or stdin.

flags.desc: Address of the domain controller (KDC). If not set, it is looked up via DNS.
Fully qualified domain name (e.g. contoso.com). One of --dc/-d is required.
Number of threads (default 10).
Delay between attempts (ms); enables single-threaded mode.
Abort if a locked account is detected (protection against lockout).
Write the log to a file.
Log failures and errors (only successes by default).
Force weak encryption (arcfour-hmac-md5).
File with a list of usernames to check (positional).
File with a list of users (1st positional).
A single password to test against all (2nd positional).
bruteuser: password wordlist (1st positional), then the username (2nd).
bruteforce: file with user:password pairs (or '-' for stdin).

recipes.note: Check which names from the wordlist are valid domain accounts.
Spray a single password across all users (with --safe against lockout).
Brute-force passwords from a wordlist against a single user.
Brute-force ready-made user:password pairs from a file.

tools.notes: You must specify the domain (-d) and preferably --dc (otherwise the KDC is resolved via DNS). userenum does not trigger the failed-login event 4625 - the quietest way to validate names. For spray always add --safe to avoid locking out accounts. Usernames are sensitive to existence but do not cause lockout during userenum.

tools.title: responder
tools.purpose: A poisoner for name resolution protocols (LLMNR, NBT-NS, MDNS) with built-in rogue HTTP/SMB/MSSQL/LDAP servers. Captures NTLMv1/v2 hashes from victims' broadcast queries for subsequent cracking or relay.

flags.desc: Network interface to listen on (required).
Analysis mode - only observe requests, do NOT respond (passive recon).
Verbose output.
Quiet mode.
Enable the rogue WPAD proxy (capture browser HTTP authentication).
Force authentication for WPAD (rogue PAC requires login).
Force authentication on the proxy (together with -w).
Respond to NetBIOS domain suffix queries.
Respond to NetBIOS wredir queries (careful - noisy).
Enable rogue DHCP responses (DHCPv4) with WPAD/DNS injection.
Enable DHCPv6 poisoning (mitm6-like).
Serve Basic HTTP authentication instead of NTLM (cleartext passwords).
Force a downgrade to LM hashes (easier to crack) on old clients.
Disable Extended Session Security (NTLMv1 downgrade).
Return an error code instead of an authentication challenge (force a retry).
Upstream proxy for captured traffic.

recipes.note: Passive analysis: see who is sending LLMNR/NBT-NS without poisoning anything.
Basic LLMNR/NBT-NS/MDNS poisoning with NTLM hash collection.
Poisoning + rogue WPAD proxy + responses to domain suffix.
Capture Basic authentication (cleartext passwords, not hashes).

tools.notes: The interface is set via -I; there is no separate positional target (the entire broadcast segment is poisoned). Captured hashes and logs: /usr/share/responder/logs/ (in hashcat format). Config is /etc/responder/Responder.conf (specific servers are enabled/disabled there). Before a relay attack, disable the SMB/HTTP servers in the config and use ntlmrelayx. Run as root.

tools.title: evil-winrm
tools.purpose: A full-featured shell for remote access to Windows over WinRM (5985/5986). Supports Pass-the-Hash, Kerberos, file upload/download, .NET assembly execution, and AMSI bypass. The standard after obtaining credentials with WinRM access.

flags.desc: Target IP or hostname (required).
Port (default 5985; with -S becomes 5986).
Enable SSL/TLS (port 5986).
Endpoint URL (default /wsman).
Username.
Password.
NT hash for Pass-the-Hash (instead of a password).
Realm for Kerberos authentication.
Kerberos ticket (ccache or kirbi).
SPN prefix for Kerberos (default HTTP).
Public certificate for certificate-based authentication.
Private key for certificate-based authentication.
Custom connection User-Agent.
Path to local PowerShell scripts (loaded by invoking them by name).
Path to local C#/.NET assemblies (for Invoke-Binary).
Log the session.
Disable remote path autocompletion (if it is slow).
Upload a file from the attacker to the target.
Download a file from the target.
Execute a .NET assembly in memory (from the -e directory).
Patch AMSI in memory.
List services and rights on them.
Show loaded functions/commands.

recipes.note: Basic connection with a username and password.
Pass-the-Hash - login with an NT hash without a password.
Connect over WinRM with SSL (port 5986).
With local PS scripts and .NET assemblies loaded for post-exploitation.

tools.notes: The target is set with the -i flag (not positionally). Access requires the user to be a member of the Remote Management Users group (or an admin). Inside the shell, upload/download, Invoke-Binary, Bypass-4MSI, Dll-Loader, and Donut-Loader are available. For Kerberos you need a valid ticket in KRB5CCNAME and -r with a realm. SSL (-S) switches the port to 5986.

tools.title: bloodhound / bloodhound-python
tools.subcategory: data collection for the AD graph
tools.purpose: bloodhound-python (BloodHound.py) is a Python ingestor: it collects users, groups, sessions, ACLs, trusts, and attack paths from the domain into JSON for import into BloodHound. Does not require running on Windows - works from Linux over the network.

flags.desc: DNS server (usually the DC itself) - critical for resolving domain names.
Name (FQDN) of the domain controller for queries.
Global catalog server.
Use TCP for DNS queries.
Disable Global Catalog auto-detection (--disable-autogc).
Username (UPN).
Password.
NT hash (or LM:NT) for Pass-the-Hash.
Authenticate via Kerberos.
Domain name (required).
Use LDAPS (636) instead of LDAP.
Collection methods: Default, All, Group, LocalAdmin, Session, Trusts, ACL, ObjectProps, Container, RDP, DCOM, PSRemote, LoggedOn, DCOnly.
Number of worker threads (--workers).
Authentication method (auto/ntlm/kerberos).
Prefix for output file names (--outputprefix).
Pack the collected JSON into a single zip archive (handy for import).
Verbose output.

recipes.note: Full data collection and packing into a zip for import into BloodHound.

recipes.cmd: bloodhound-python -u user@domain.local -p pass -d domain.local -dc dc01.domain.local -ns {TARGET} -c DCOnly
recipes.note: Quiet collection via the DC only (without contacting every host).
Collection via Pass-the-Hash without a password.
Collection via Kerberos and LDAPS.

tools.notes: -ns is REQUIRED and must point to the domain DNS (usually the DC), otherwise names will not resolve. -c All collects everything except LoggedOn; DCOnly is the quietest (only LDAP queries to the DC). For BloodHound CE install bloodhound-ce-python (the output format differs from legacy). Import: the zip archive via the web interface. The Windows alternative is SharpHound.

tools.title: certipy
tools.purpose: A tool for enumerating and exploiting AD Certificate Services. Finds vulnerable certificate templates (ESC1-16), requests and forges certificates, performs NTLM relay and Shadow Credentials for privilege escalation and account takeover.

modes.name: find
modes.desc: Enumerate CAs and templates, find ESC vulnerabilities.

modes.name: req
modes.desc: Request a certificate by template from the CA.

modes.name: auth
modes.desc: Authenticate with a certificate (PKINIT) to get a TGT/NT hash.

modes.name: relay
modes.desc: NTLM relay to an AD CS HTTP/RPC endpoint (ESC8/ESC11).

modes.name: shadow
modes.desc: Shadow Credentials - account takeover via msDS-KeyCredentialLink.

modes.name: forge
modes.desc: Forge a certificate when the CA private key is available (Golden Cert).

flags.desc: Username (UPN).
Password.
NT hash (or LM:NT) for Pass-the-Hash.
Authenticate via Kerberos.
Do not prompt for a password (with -k or when anonymous).
Domain controller IP.
DNS server (usually the DC).
Target host (CA/server) to connect to.
Show only vulnerable templates (ESC).
Only enabled (published) templates.
Print the result to the console instead of files.
Export for BloodHound.
Hide admin rights in the output (less noise).
Name of the certification authority (CA).
Certificate template name.
Inject a UPN into the SAN (ESC1 - impersonate another user).
Inject a DNS name into the SAN.
Inject a SID into the certificate.
Application policies (for ESC15).
Filename prefix for saving the .pfx.
The .pfx certificate file for authentication.
Username for certificate-based authentication.
Domain for authentication.
Open an LDAP shell after authentication.
Target account for Shadow Credentials.
CA certificate+key for forging (Golden Certificate).
Subject of the forged certificate.
Serial number of the forged certificate.
Relay target (protocol://host) for ESC8/ESC11.
Template to request during relay.

recipes.note: Find vulnerable certificate templates (ESC) and print to the console.

recipes.cmd: certipy req -u user@domain.local -p pass -dc-ip {TARGET} -ca DOMAIN-CA -template VulnTemplate -upn administrator@domain.local
recipes.note: ESC1: request a certificate with the UPN spoofed to an admin.
Authenticate with the certificate and obtain a TGT + the admin's NT hash.

recipes.cmd: certipy shadow auto -u user@domain.local -p pass -dc-ip {TARGET} -account target$
recipes.note: Shadow Credentials: take over an account and obtain its NT hash automatically.
Forge a Golden Certificate when the CA key is available.

tools.notes: The first argument is the subcommand (find/req/auth/relay/shadow/forge/ca/cert/account/template/parse). Flags use a SINGLE hyphen (-dc-ip, -no-pass) in impacket style. The classic ESC1 path: find -vulnerable -> req with -upn -> auth. shadow auto does add+auth+clear automatically. For relay (ESC8) DC-ip is not needed - the target is set via -target.

tools.title: hydra
tools.purpose: A fast parallel brute-forcer for network services: SSH, FTP, RDP, SMB, HTTP forms, databases, and dozens of other protocols. Brute-forces logins and passwords from wordlists against a service on the target.

flags.desc: A single login.
File with a list of logins.
A single password.
File with a list of passwords.
File with login:password pairs (instead of -L/-P).
Extra checks: n - empty password, s - password=login, r - reversed login.
Iterate logins first, then passwords (the default is the other way around).
Non-standard service port.
Use SSL for the connection.
File with a list of targets (mass attack).
IPv4 only.
IPv6 only.
Number of parallel tasks per target (for SSH lower it to ~4).
Total number of parallel connects (with -M).
Response wait timeout (seconds).
Stop after the first valid login:password pair on a host.
Stop on the first success on any of the hosts (with -M).
Write found credentials to a file.
Output file format: text, json, jsonv1.
Show every login:password attempt (very verbose).
Verbose output.
Restore an interrupted session from hydra.restore.
Ignore the restore file (start over).
Generate passwords: min:max:charset (a-letters, 1-digits, !-special characters).

recipes.note: Brute-force SSH for a single user (few threads so it does not fail).
Brute-force login/password pairs over SSH, stop on the first success.
Brute-force an HTTP form: ^USER^/^PASS^ are fields, F= is the failure marker string.
Password spray a single password across a user list over SMB.
Brute-force MySQL on a non-standard port.

tools.notes: Syntax: hydra [options] target service [module parameters]. The target is positional (second to last argument), the service is last (ssh, ftp, smb, rdp, mysql, http-post-form, http-get...). For http-post-form the format is 'path:body:marker': ^USER^/^PASS^ are placeholders, F=string is the failure marker, S=string is the success marker. For SSH keep -t <= 4. Module help: hydra -U http-post-form.
